✦ LNG4 · UNIT 1
Oral Communication
Grade 4 Language (2023) — Oral Communication, Reading, Writing, Media Literacy
📅 September · Weeks 1–4

Students explore what it truly means to listen — not just hear — through modelled examples and structured practice activities.

Learning Objectives

  • Identify features of effective listening (OME: 1.3)
  • Demonstrate attentive body language and verbal acknowledgement cues
  • Distinguish listening for information from listening for enjoyment

Lesson Content

Active listening is a skill that requires deliberate practice. When we truly listen, we make eye contact with the speaker, keep our body still and facing them, and focus our mind on their words rather than planning our own response. These habits signal respect and lead to better comprehension.

There are three distinct types of listening. Listening for information means capturing specific facts or instructions accurately — like following a recipe or taking notes from a teacher. Listening for enjoyment means relaxing into a story or piece of music without needing to remember every detail. Listening critically means evaluating whether an argument makes sense and whether the evidence is convincing.

Good listeners use two active strategies to show they understood. Paraphrasing means restating the speaker's idea in your own words: 'So what you mean is...' Clarifying questions invite the speaker to explain more: 'Can you give me an example of that?' Both strategies strengthen understanding and make the speaker feel heard.

Differentiation Notes
Below grade level: Draw or sketch while listening as a processing support tool
On grade level: Standard lesson — practise pair listening with structured feedback
Above grade level: Peer-coach a classmate and provide written observations of their listening behaviours

Students learn to adapt their voice, pace, and vocabulary to suit different audiences and speaking purposes.

Learning Objectives

  • Use appropriate volume, tone, and pace (OME: 1.4)
  • Organize ideas using a speaking frame before presenting
  • Respond to questions and comments respectfully and on topic

Lesson Content

Every speaking situation requires us to make choices about HOW we communicate, not just WHAT we say. Volume means how loud or soft we speak — a library whisper versus an outdoor announcement. Tone conveys emotion — encouraging, serious, playful. Pace means how fast or slow we speak — rushing loses the audience, speaking too slowly loses their attention.

Different contexts demand different speaking approaches. Presenting to the class requires clear structure, eye contact, and a projected voice. Explaining something to a younger student calls for simpler vocabulary and a slower pace. Chatting with a friend is informal and spontaneous. Skilled speakers shift naturally between these modes.

Before speaking in any formal situation, use a simple planning frame: 'My main point is... My supporting reasons are... My conclusion is...' This three-part structure prevents rambling, helps the audience follow your logic, and signals confidence. Even thirty seconds of planning dramatically improves the quality of spoken communication.

Differentiation Notes
Below grade level: Provide a sentence-frame card to reference while speaking
On grade level: Record yourself speaking for 60 seconds and self-assess using three criteria
Above grade level: Debate a classmate on an assigned topic using formal speaking conventions and structured turns

Students practise the art of respectful disagreement — building on others' ideas and expressing reasoned opinions with supporting evidence.

Learning Objectives

  • Follow agreed-upon rules for discussion (OME: 1.6)
  • Build on classmates' ideas using 'I agree/disagree because...' structures
  • Express an opinion with at least two supporting reasons

Lesson Content

A productive discussion is like a game of catch — each participant listens fully before throwing their contribution into the conversation, and everyone aims at the same shared goal of understanding. Effective discussants use phrases like 'Building on what you said...' or 'I see it differently because...' to show they were genuinely listening before they responded.

Debate is structured disagreement with a specific format. Two positions are assigned — students may argue a view they do not personally hold. The goal is not to 'win' but to practise reasoning with evidence, anticipating counterarguments, and expressing ideas under pressure. These are skills every profession requires.

Discussion agreements — sometimes called norms or ground rules — make conversation safe and productive. Common norms include: one person speaks at a time, all ideas are treated with respect, we challenge ideas, not people, we use evidence rather than emotion alone. When students co-create these norms, they are far more likely to honour them.

✦ Tarbiyah Checkpoint  ,Adl — Justice in Speech — عدل

When we speak, we must be fair, honest, and careful. Islam teaches that our words are a form of Amanah — a trust. We are accountable for the impact our words have on others' hearts, whether we intended harm or not. Speaking with Adl means choosing truthful, measured, and fair words even when it is difficult.

Surah An-Nisa 4:135 — 'Be persistently standing firm in justice, as witnesses for Allah'

See also: Math Unit 1 Lesson 3 — Adl in Division

Differentiation Notes
Below grade level: Provide a discussion sentence-starter card with six prompts to choose from
On grade level: Standard discussion — use a physical talking piece to enforce turn-taking
Above grade level: Take on the 'devil's advocate' role: argue the opposite of your genuine view for the full discussion

Preview — Unit 1 Quiz — Oral Communication

  1. What is the most important habit of an active listener?
    • Preparing your own response while the other person speaks
    • Making eye contact, focusing fully, and not interrupting ✓
    • Writing down every word the speaker says
    • Nodding continuously throughout the conversation
  2. 'Pace' when speaking refers to:
    • How loudly or softly you speak
    • The emotion conveyed through your voice
    • How fast or slow you speak ✓
    • The specific words you choose
  3. When disagreeing in a group discussion, the best approach is:
    • Interrupt immediately to correct the error
    • Listen fully, then respond with evidence and respect ✓
    • Stay silent to avoid conflict
    • Write a note instead of speaking aloud
  4. A 'discussion norm' is:
    • A mark given for speaking skills
    • An agreed-upon behaviour that makes discussion safe and productive ✓
    • A rule set only by the teacher
    • A formal debate format
  5. 'Paraphrasing' a speaker means:
    • Repeating their exact words back to them
    • Ignoring their point and making your own
    • Restating their idea in your own words to confirm understanding ✓
    • Disagreeing politely with their argument

Assignment Instructions
Prepare and deliver a 3-minute oral presentation on a topic of your choice. Required: (1) A hook opening that grabs attention, (2) Two main points each supported by one fact or example, (3) A conclusion restating your main idea. Use cue cards — do not read word for word. Your teacher will assess using the rubric below.

Discussion Prompt

Think of a time when someone's words made you feel really good — or really hurt. What made the difference? How can we use our words more carefully every day?
Facilitation Notes for Teacher
Encourage personal reflection. Ask students to share their own story, not someone else's. Connect to Tarbiyah: our words are an Amanah (trust) and we are accountable for their impact on others' hearts, whether we intended harm or not. Sensitive topic — model openness carefully.

✦ LNG4 · UNIT 2
Reading Comprehension
Grade 4 Language (2023) — Oral Communication, Reading, Writing, Media Literacy
📅 October · Weeks 5–8

Students build a toolkit of before, during, and after reading strategies that strong readers use automatically.

Learning Objectives

  • Apply prediction, questioning, and making connections (OME: 2.3)
  • Identify main idea and key supporting details
  • Monitor comprehension and apply fix-up strategies when meaning breaks down

Lesson Content

Strong readers are active readers. Before reading, they preview the title, headings, and images to make predictions about content. During reading, they ask questions like 'Why did this happen?' and 'What does this word mean?' and 'Does this make sense?' After reading, they summarize the key ideas and reflect on what they learned or felt.

Making connections deepens comprehension in three directions. Text-to-self connections link the reading to your own life experiences. Text-to-text connections compare this text to another book, article, or film you know. Text-to-world connections link the reading to current events or broader human experience. All three types strengthen memory and meaning.

When comprehension breaks down — when you reach the end of a paragraph and realize you have no idea what you just read — fix-up strategies help. Re-read the confusing section more slowly. Look for context clues in the surrounding sentences. Look up unfamiliar vocabulary. Break a long sentence into its parts. Struggling through confusion is normal and productive — it is not a signal to give up.

Differentiation Notes
Below grade level: Use a before/during/after reading graphic organizer with sentence starters
On grade level: Standard lesson with guided practice text and partner strategy-sharing
Above grade level: Keep a metacognitive reading log tracking every strategy used across a full book

Students discover that different texts are built differently — and that recognizing structure helps them read more efficiently and purposefully.

Learning Objectives

  • Identify and describe common text forms: fiction, non-fiction, poetry, procedural (OME: 2.1)
  • Recognize text features: headings, captions, sidebars, glossary, index
  • Compare the organizational structure of fiction and non-fiction texts

Lesson Content

Fiction texts tell stories about imaginary or imagined events and characters. They follow a narrative structure: setting, characters, conflict, rising action, climax, resolution. Non-fiction texts communicate factual information about real subjects. They use organizational patterns like compare and contrast, cause and effect, problem and solution, or chronological sequence.

Non-fiction texts use specialized features to help readers navigate efficiently. A table of contents lists what is inside and where. Headings divide the text into clearly labelled sections. Captions explain photographs and diagrams. A glossary defines technical vocabulary. An index helps you locate specific information without reading the whole text. Recognizing these features is a reading skill in itself.

Reading poetry requires a different stance than reading prose. Poets compress meaning into fewer words, use line breaks and white space deliberately, and often communicate through images and sound rather than direct statement. Reading a poem once is almost never enough — each re-reading reveals new layers of meaning that were invisible the first time through.

Differentiation Notes
Below grade level: Sort a set of printed text samples by type using visual sorting cards with a partner
On grade level: Standard lesson — complete a text features scavenger hunt in a non-fiction book
Above grade level: Create a multi-text comparison chart across three different text forms on the same topic

Students move beyond understanding what a text says to questioning why it was written and whose perspective it represents.

Learning Objectives

  • Identify the author's purpose: inform, persuade, entertain (OME: 2.7)
  • Distinguish verifiable facts from debatable opinions
  • Make inferences using text evidence combined with prior knowledge

Lesson Content

Every text has an author who made deliberate choices — what information to include, what to leave out, whose perspective to centre, and which words to use. Critical readers ask questions beyond comprehension: Who wrote this and why? Who benefits from this perspective? What is left unsaid? Could a different author tell this same story very differently?

Facts are statements that can be verified — checked against evidence. 'Canada has ten provinces' is a fact that can be confirmed. Opinions are statements that express a personal view or judgment. 'Hockey is the best sport' cannot be proven true or false for everyone. The skill of distinguishing facts from opinions is essential for evaluating news, advertising, political speech, and social media.

Inference is reading between the lines. A strong inference combines two ingredients: a specific clue from the text plus background knowledge the reader brings to the text. Weak inferences rely entirely on one or the other. The habit of writing 'The text says... + I know that... = So I infer...' makes the thinking visible and testable.

✦ Tarbiyah Checkpoint  ,Amanah — Trustworthiness with Information — أمانة

Not everything we read or share online is true or fair. The Quran explicitly commands believers to verify information before acting on it or passing it along. Sharing unverified news — even without the intention to deceive — is a breach of Amanah. Our responsibility to truth does not stop at our own words, it extends to every piece of information we amplify.

Surah Al-Hujurat 49:6 — 'O believers, if an evildoer brings you any news, verify it so you do not harm people out of ignorance'

See also: Science Unit 1 Lesson 3 — Amanah as Earth Stewards

Differentiation Notes
Below grade level: Use a two-column organizer: 'The text says / I know / So I infer' with sentence starters
On grade level: Standard lesson — short paired-text comparison with a fact/opinion sorting activity
Above grade level: Write a counter-argument to an opinion piece, citing evidence from a second independent source

Preview — Unit 2 Quiz — Reading Comprehension

  1. A 'text-to-self' connection means:
    • Comparing two books written on the same topic
    • Connecting something in the text to an experience from your own life ✓
    • Finding the author's main argument
    • Identifying the text form and its features
  2. Which of the following is a text FEATURE (not a text form)?
    • Fiction
    • Non-fiction
    • Poetry
    • A glossary at the back of the book ✓
  3. The MAIN IDEA of a non-fiction text is:
    • The first sentence of the text
    • The longest paragraph in the text
    • The central message the author most wants to communicate ✓
    • The title alone
  4. A FACT differs from an OPINION because:
    • Facts are always found in textbooks
    • Opinions are more interesting to read
    • Facts can be verified with evidence, opinions express a personal view ✓
    • Opinions are always wrong
  5. Making an INFERENCE means:
    • Copying the main idea directly from the text
    • Guessing randomly without using the text at all
    • Using specific text clues plus prior knowledge to figure out something the author did not directly state ✓
    • Summarizing only the ending of the text

Assignment Instructions
Read a non-fiction book of your choice on any topic. Write a one-page response (minimum three paragraphs) that includes: (1) The main idea in your own words, (2) Three specific supporting details from the text, (3) One inference you made with the text evidence that supports it, (4) Your opinion of the book with two clear reasons. Label each statement as fact or opinion.

Discussion Prompt

Has a book ever changed the way you think about something — even slightly? Describe the book and explain what shifted in your thinking. If no book, what other text — article, video, or speech — changed your mind about something?
Facilitation Notes for Teacher
Accept all text types equally — picture books, chapter books, articles, speeches, even song lyrics. Islamic connection: the Prophet ﷺ said 'A word of wisdom is the lost property of the believer — wherever they find it, they have the most right to it.' Knowledge transforms us when we encounter it with an open mind.

✦ LNG4 · UNIT 3
Writing
Grade 4 Language (2023) — Oral Communication, Reading, Writing, Media Literacy
📅 November–December · Weeks 9–12

Students discover that strong writing is never written once — it is a recursive, multi-stage process of planning, drafting, revising, editing, and publishing.

Learning Objectives

  • Apply the five stages of the writing process (OME: 3.1)
  • Use a graphic organizer to plan before drafting
  • Distinguish revising (improving ideas) from editing (fixing conventions)

Lesson Content

Professional writers — journalists, novelists, scientists, and lawyers — all follow a writing process even when it is not labelled as such. The five stages are: (1) Prewriting: brainstorm, gather ideas, and organize using a graphic organizer. (2) Drafting: get all ideas down without stopping to perfect anything. (3) Revising: improve the ideas, organization, voice, and word choice. (4) Editing: correct spelling, grammar, and punctuation. (5) Publishing: produce a clean final copy to share with an audience.

Prewriting tools match the writing task. Mind maps (ideas radiating from a central topic) work well for any form. Story maps (setting, characters, problem, events, resolution) support narrative writing. T-charts or Venn diagrams support comparison writing. Numbered outlines support research and opinion writing. Choosing the right organizer before drafting saves significant time and produces stronger first drafts.

The most common student mistake is treating editing as the same as revising. Revising is large-scale improvement: Does my argument make sense? Is this paragraph in the right place? Do I need more evidence here? Should I cut this section? Editing is surface-level correction: Is this word spelled correctly? Does this sentence need a comma? Editing before revising wastes effort — you may polish sentences that will ultimately be cut.

Differentiation Notes
Below grade level: Use a pre-filled graphic organizer with key vocabulary, allow dictation for the drafting stage
On grade level: Standard process writing — complete all five stages on a one-paragraph piece
Above grade level: Write a detailed process reflection explaining every revision decision made and why

Students learn to write well-structured paragraphs — the fundamental building block of all formal writing across every subject.

Learning Objectives

  • Write paragraphs with a topic sentence, three supporting details, and a concluding sentence (OME: 3.3)
  • Use at least three transition words within the paragraph
  • Self-evaluate a paragraph against a clear, specific checklist

Lesson Content

A strong paragraph has four components working together. The topic sentence announces the main idea clearly — it tells the reader exactly what this paragraph will be about, and nothing else. Three supporting sentences provide evidence, examples, facts, or explanations that prove or develop the topic sentence. Transition words (first, also, in addition, however, finally) connect ideas smoothly. The concluding sentence wraps up the paragraph's argument and often links back to the topic sentence.

Transition words are the glue of effective writing. Sequence transitions (first, then, next, finally) organize steps and events. Addition transitions (also, furthermore, in addition, moreover) build arguments point by point. Contrast transitions (however, on the other hand, although, yet) signal a shift in direction or a complication. Cause-and-effect transitions (therefore, as a result, consequently) show how one idea leads to another.

A paragraph self-evaluation checklist: Does the topic sentence state one clear, specific idea? Are there at least three supporting details? Does each supporting sentence directly relate to the topic sentence — no straying? Are transitions present between ideas? Does the concluding sentence complete the thought without simply repeating the topic sentence word for word? Apply this checklist every time before moving to the next paragraph.

Differentiation Notes
Below grade level: Provide a paragraph frame: 'My topic is _. First, _. Also, _. Finally, _. In conclusion, _.'
On grade level: Standard paragraph writing — write three connected paragraphs on a self-chosen topic
Above grade level: Write a multi-paragraph essay with a clear thesis, three body paragraphs each with topic and concluding sentences, and a conclusion paragraph

Students explore two powerful forms of writing — personal narratives that capture experience, and persuasive writing that changes minds.

Learning Objectives

  • Write a personal narrative with a clear problem and meaningful resolution (OME: 3.2)
  • Write a persuasive paragraph with a claim and two supporting reasons
  • Use descriptive language and precise word choice to strengthen both forms

Lesson Content

Personal narratives draw readers into your lived experience using specific sensory details, authentic dialogue, and your distinctive voice. Strong narratives share four elements: a hook opening that creates immediate engagement, rising action that builds tension through a central problem, a climax — the most important moment of decision or change, and a resolution that shows what happened and what was learned. Vague narratives summarize events. Powerful narratives show them.

Persuasive writing begins with a clear, specific, debatable claim: 'Our school should have a longer lunch break because...' or 'Students benefit from learning a second language from an early age because...' Then it builds the argument using evidence (statistics, expert opinion, specific examples), acknowledges counterarguments to show fairness, and concludes by restating the position powerfully and calling for action.

Word choice is the single biggest difference between flat writing and vivid writing. 'She walked slowly' communicates a fact. 'She shuffled forward, shoulders caved, each step weighted with reluctance' creates an image. Replace empty adjectives (good, bad, nice, big) with precise ones (generous, devastating, thoughtful, towering). Replace weak verbs (go, do, say) with specific ones (trudge, accomplish, insist). Every revision should ask: is there a more precise word for this?

✦ Tarbiyah Checkpoint  ,Ihsan — Excellence in Our Work — إحسان

Allah loves excellence in everything we do — including our writing. When we put sincere, thorough effort into a piece of writing — planning carefully, revising honestly, choosing words with precision — this is Ihsan. Our writing is a reflection of our character and our respect for the people who will read it. Half-effort produces half-work. Full effort is a form of gratitude for the gift of language.

Hadith: 'Allah loves that when any of you does a task, they do it with excellence.' (Al-Bayhaqi)

See also: Arts Unit 1 Lesson 3 — Ihsan as Beauty in Creative Work

Differentiation Notes
Below grade level: Provide a narrative story map: Hook / Setting / Problem / Events / Resolution / What I Learned
On grade level: Standard dual-form lesson — write one personal narrative paragraph and one persuasive paragraph
Above grade level: Write a persuasive letter to a real, named audience — the principal, a city councillor — and discuss actually sending it

Preview — Unit 3 Quiz — Writing

  1. The FIRST step in the writing process is:
    • Editing for spelling and punctuation
    • Publishing the final clean copy
    • Prewriting — brainstorming and organizing ideas before drafting ✓
    • Drafting quickly without stopping to perfect anything
  2. REVISING your writing means:
    • Checking and correcting spelling and punctuation
    • Typing up the final polished copy
    • Improving your ideas, organization, voice, and word choice ✓
    • Reading your work aloud once
  3. A TOPIC SENTENCE in a paragraph should:
    • Come at the very end of the paragraph as a surprise
    • Be the longest, most detailed sentence in the paragraph
    • State the single main idea of the whole paragraph clearly ✓
    • Always begin with the word 'I'
  4. Which sentence uses MORE PRECISE word choice?
    • The dog was very big and ran really fast
    • The Great Dane thundered across the yard in three strides ✓
    • My dog is good and I like him a lot
    • I saw a large dog moving quickly
  5. In PERSUASIVE writing, a 'counter-argument' is:
    • Your central claim or position
    • Additional evidence that supports your own view
    • An argument from the opposing side that you acknowledge and then address ✓
    • The conclusion that restates your position
